questionText: An arc $C$ is given by $x = t^{2}$ and $y = 4t$ for $0 \le t \le 4$. The line $l$ has equation $y = 2x - 6$. Find the coordinates of every point where $C$ meets $l$, making clear why any solution of the quadratic is rejected.

steps.workingLatex: x = t^{2}, \quad y = 4t, \quad 0 \le t \le 4, \qquad l:\ y = 2x - 6
steps.explanation: Intersections occur where the curve's coordinates also satisfy the line. Substituting the parametric expressions gives one equation in $t$.

steps.description: Substitute the parametric coordinates into the line
steps.explanation: Replacing $x$ by $t^{2}$ and $y$ by $4t$ in $y = 2x - 6$ turns the condition into an algebraic equation in $t$ alone.
Bringing all terms to one side produces a quadratic set equal to zero, ready to be solved.

steps.description: Divide through by the common factor
steps.explanation: Every coefficient is even, so dividing by $2$ gives a simpler quadratic with the same roots.
The simplified quadratic factorises neatly, exposing the two candidate parameter values.
Each factor gives one value of $t$. These are only candidates until we check them against the allowed range.
The arc is defined only for $t$ between $0$ and $4$, so a root is meaningful only if it lies in this interval.

steps.description: Test each root against the range
steps.explanation: Only $t = 3$ falls inside the permitted interval; $t = -1$ is outside it, so it cannot correspond to a point on this arc.

steps.description: State the range of x on the arc
steps.explanation: As $t$ runs from $0$ to $4$, the value $x = t^{2}$ sweeps from $0$ to $16$; this describes exactly which part of the parabola is drawn.

steps.description: Find the coordinates for the accepted root
steps.explanation: Substituting the valid parameter $t = 3$ back into the parametric equations gives the actual intersection point.

steps.description: Verify the point lies on the line
steps.explanation: Checking $x = 9$ in $y = 2x - 6$ returns $12$, confirming the point really is on the line.

steps.description: Explain why the rejected root gives no point
steps.explanation: The rejected root would give the point $(1,\,-4)$; although its $x$-value lies in $[0,16]$, the parameter $t = -1$ is not in the allowed interval, so this point is not part of the drawn arc.
Collecting the surviving result gives the single admissible intersection.
Because only one root satisfies the parameter restriction, the arc meets the line at exactly one point.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: The arc meets the line at the single point $(9,\, 12)$ (from $t = 3$). The other root $t = -1$ is rejected because it lies outside the parameter range $0 \le t \le 4$.
